findings: Destination Port feature exhibits stable, strong distributional divergence between BENIGN and DDoS classes across multiple time windows within evidence e33.
DDoS class distribution is dominated by a single port value with near-zero entropy, indicating a shortcut-like signal.
Temporal distribution patterns are stable but may be influenced by moderate class imbalance, raising uncertainty about broader generalizability.

findings: Destination Port feature shows strong distributional divergence between BENIGN and DDoS classes with low entropy and a dominant port in DDoS.
Cardinality verification confirms low-cardinality behavior consistent with dominant port usage.
Moderate class imbalance introduces uncertainty about the stability and generalizability of the distributional divergence.
contradictions: Strong distributional divergence may be confounded by class imbalance, preserving local tension.
limitations: Class imbalance impact on distributional divergence stability remains unresolved.

findings: Destination Port feature exhibits strong distributional divergence between BENIGN and DDoS classes, with DDoS dominated by a single port and near-zero entropy.
No evidence of narrow structural dependency clusters was found for Destination Port, indicating the divergence is distributional rather than dependency-driven.
An unresolved tension remains about the impact of moderate class imbalance on the stability and generalizability of this distributional signal.
